Associations of Jesuits responsible for the collection and publication of the Acta Sanctorum (Lives of the Saints of the Christian Church, 1643-1794).

Founder of Islam, whose prophetic teachings, encompassing political and social as well as religious principles, became the basis of Islamic civilization and have had a vast influence on world history.
Any of the Aramaic translations, more or less literal, of portions of the Old Testament used in the synagogues of Palestine and Babylonia.
Philosopher, theologian, and one of the earliest apologists of the Christian church who sought to reconcile Christian doctrine and pagan culture.
The first important Christian ecclesiastical writer in Latin (160?-220?), whose work is remarkable for its blunt sarcasm, epigrammatic phrasing, and aggressive partisan spirit.
In medieval Scholastic philosophy, doctrine stating that abstractions, known as universals, are without essential or substantive reality, and that only individual objects have real existence.
In this book, Austrian-British philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein argued that 'philosophy is a battle against the bewitchment of our intelligence by means of language.'

In the tradition of antiquity, a large island in the Western Ocean, near the Pillars of Hercules, which in the earliest accounts is said to have been engulfed by the ocean as a result of an earthquake.
English clergyman and hymnist, born in the rectory at Epworth, Lincolnshire and educated at Westminster School and Christ Church, University of Oxford.

In the history of the Christian church, term used to refer to both the break between the Eastern and Western churches, traditionally dated 1054, and the period (1378-1417) in the Western church when two popes simultaneously claimed to be legitimate.
In philosophy, a system of thought that emphasizes the role of reason in obtaining knowledge, in contrast to empiricism, which emphasizes the role of experience, especially sense perception.
Book of the Old Testament, one of those constituting the third part--the Writings--of the Hebrew canon (Bible); it was one of the last books to be accepted as canonical by both Jews and Christians.
A religious festival, also known as Hallowmas, that is celebrated on November 1st in the Anglican and Roman Catholic traditions.
Book of the Old Testament in those versions of the Bible following the Greek Septuagint (generally Roman Catholic and Orthodox versions).
In theology and philosophy, attempts by a group of scholars and church officials to reinterpret Christian doctrine in terms of the scientific thought of the 19th century.

Word coined by the 5th-century biblical scholar Saint Jerome for the biblical books received by the church of his time as part of the Greek version of the Old Testament, but that were not included in the Hebrew Bible.

In ethical philosophy, theology, law, and social theory, a set of principles, based on what are assumed to be the permanent characteristics of human nature, that can serve as a standard for evaluating conduct and civil laws.
Members of a medieval Christian sect that criticized the Roman Catholic Church and advocated lives of preaching and poverty, and banned as heretical by the Church.
French Roman Catholic existentialist philosopher, dramatist, and critic who insisted that individuals can only be understood as embodied and involved in specific situations.
Austrian-British philosopher (1889-1951), one of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, particularly noted for his contribution to analytic and linguistic philosophy.

Great 16th-century religious revolution in the Christian church, which ended the ecclesiastical supremacy of the pope in Western Christendom and resulted in the establishment of the Protestant churches.
Austrian-British philosopher (1889-1951), one of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, particularly noted for his contribution to analytic and linguistic philosophy.
The most famous French exponent of Jansenism, a 17th- and 18th-century Roman Catholic theological movement of rigorous and unorthodox tendencies.
Festival celebrated on November 1 in the Roman Catholic and Anglican churches, and by the Orthodox churches on the first Sunday after Pentecost, in honor of God and all his saints.
British philosopher, born in Sydney, Australia, and educated in Melbourne and at the University of Oxford; one of the few 20th century philosophers to develop a comprehensive metaphysical system.
Austrian-British philosopher, who was one of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, particularly noted for his contribution to the movement known as analytic and linguistic philosophy.

In ecclesiastical history, a combination of theological doctrines and political positions supporting the relative independence of the French Roman Catholic church and the French government in their relations with the pope.
Jewish mysticism in all its forms; specifically, the esoteric theosophy that crystallized in 13th-century Spain and Provence, France, around Sefer ha-zohar (The Book of Splendor).

Medieval European theological movement that attempted to use natural human reason, in particular the philosophy and science of Aristotle, to understand the supernatural content of Christian revelation.
Called Apostle to the Indians (1604-90), American clergyman, born in Widford, Hertfordshire, England, and educated at the University of Cambridge.

Protestant denomination organized in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1957 as the result of the union of the General Council of the Congregational Christian Churches (Congregationalism) and the Evangelical and Reformed Church.

Two books of the New Testament, classified among the seven Epistles designated as Catholic (or General) by the early church because they are addressed to Christians generally, instead of particular churches.
